attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of lower abdominal pain and inability to pass urine. He has a medical history of benign prostatic hyperplasia and hypertension. His medications include tamsulosin and amlodipine.

On examination, he appears uncomfortable. His pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. His lower abdomen is distended and tender. A bladder scan reveals 1200 mL of urine. A urinary catheter is inserted, draining 1500 mL of clear urine.

Four hours after catheter insertion, the patient has passed 2.5 L of urine. His serum creatinine is 180 µmol/L (60-110) and potassium is 3.2 mmol/L (3.5-5.0).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is experiencing post-obstructive diuresis following relief of acute urinary retention. The large urine output (2.5 L in four hours) after catheterisation, along with the elevated creatinine and low potassium, are characteristic of this condition. The most appropriate next step in management is to administer 0.9% NaCl to replace the fluid losses and prevent dehydration and further electrolyte disturbances.

Administer intravenous furosemide would be inappropriate in this situation. The patient is already experiencing significant diuresis and does not need additional stimulation of urine output. Furosemide could worsen dehydration and electrolyte imbalances.

Commence 1.5 L fluid restriction would be contraindicated in this case. The patient is losing large amounts of fluid through diuresis and needs fluid replacement, not restriction. Fluid restriction could lead to severe dehydration and worsen the patient's condition.

Perform a manual bladder washout is not necessary at this point. The catheter is functioning well, as evidenced by the large urine output. There's no indication of catheter blockage or need for irrigation.

Remove the urinary catheter would be premature and potentially harmful. The patient still needs the catheter to monitor urine output and manage the post-obstructive diuresis. Removal could lead to re-obstruction and complicate management.

Post-obstructive diuresis is a common complication following relief of prolonged urinary retention. It occurs due to the accumulation of osmotically active substances in the blood during the period of obstruction. When the obstruction is relieved, these substances promote diuresis. Management involves careful fluid replacement to match urine output, typically with 0.9% NaCl or Hartmanns, and close monitoring of electrolytes and renal function. The goal is to prevent dehydration while allowing the diuresis to resolve naturally over 24-48 hours. In some cases, potassium supplementation may also be necessary.
learningPoints: Post-obstructive diuresis can occur after relief of urinary retention, requiring careful fluid management to prevent electrolyte imbalances.
Fluid replacement is crucial in managing post-obstructive diuresis to prevent dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.
Serum creatinine and electrolytes should be closely monitored in patients with post-obstructive diuresis.
references: Leslie SW, Sajjad H, Sharma S. Postobstructive Diuresis. In: StatPearls [online]. 2020. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459387/

Halbgewachs C, Domes T. Postobstructive diuresis: pay close attention to urinary retention. Can Fam Physician. 2015 Feb;61(2):137-42. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4325860/
